title: 1D Arrays
description: Declaring, initializing, indexing, and looping over a one-dimensional array — formalizing the int[] numbers preview from Enhanced for Loop and Varargs.
objectives: Declare and initialize a 1D array in multiple ways
Access and modify elements using an index
Understand that array size is fixed once created
Loop over an array with both regular and enhanced for loops

sections.text: We previewed arrays back in Enhanced for Loop and Varargs without fully explaining them. Today we cover arrays properly.
A fixed-size, ordered collection of values of the same type, stored together in memory and accessed by a numeric index starting at 0.

sections.code: int[] numbers = new int[5]; // an array of 5 ints, all default to 0

sections.title: Pieces of int[] numbers = new int[5];
rows: The type: an array of int values
Creates an actual array in memory with room for 5 int values

sections.text: Every element starts at that type's default value (recall the Data Types lesson's default-value table) — 0 for int, until you assign something else.
This shorthand creates an array AND fills it with these 5 values in one line — Java figures out the size (5) from how many values you listed.

Array indices start at 0, not 1. numbers[0] is the FIRST element (10), and numbers[4] is the LAST element in a 5-element array, not numbers[5]. Using numbers[5] here would crash the program with an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException — there is no 6th element.

Once created, an array's size (its .length) can never change. There's no way to "add a 6th element" to a 5-element array — you'd need to create a brand new, larger array and copy values over. This limitation is exactly why the Java Collections Framework's ArrayList exists, previewed briefly at the end of this module.

i < numbers.length (not <=) is deliberate — with 5 elements, valid indices are 0 through 4, and numbers.length is 5. Using i <= numbers.length would try numbers[5] on the last pass, crashing with ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting arrays are 0-indexed — the last valid index is length - 1, not length.
Using i <= array.length in a loop condition instead of i < array.length, causing an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.
Trying to resize an array after creating it — arrays have a fixed size; you must create a new array if you need more room.
Confusing array.length (a field, no parentheses) with String's .length() (a method, with parentheses) — covered in the Strings lesson.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is an array in Java?
questions.answer: A fixed-size, ordered collection of values of the same type, stored together in memory and accessed by a zero-based numeric index.

questions.question: What is the valid index range for an array of length n?
questions.answer: 0 through n - 1. Accessing index n or beyond throws an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.

questions.question: Can an array's size change after it's created?
questions.answer: No. Java arrays have a fixed size for their entire lifetime; to "grow" one, you must create a new, larger array and copy the existing elements into it.

questions.question: What happens if you access an array at an invalid index?
questions.answer: Java throws an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException at runtime, immediately stopping normal execution unless the exception is handled (exception handling is covered later, in Advanced Java).

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: An array is a fixed-size, ordered collection of same-typed values, accessed by a zero-based index — index 0 is the first element, index length - 1 is the last. Arrays can be declared with new type[size] (defaulting every element) or initialized directly with {value, value, ...}. Once created, an array's size never changes; growing it requires creating a new array and copying values over, which is exactly the limitation that motivates ArrayList later in the Collections Framework. Looping over an array typically uses i < array.length (not <=) to avoid an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Array = a fixed row of labeled boxes, numbered starting at 0. The last box is at length - 1, never at length itself.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What is the index of the first element in an array? The last, for an array of length 5?
Can you resize an array after creating it?
Why should a loop condition use i < array.length instead of i <= array.length?
What exception is thrown for an invalid array index?

sections.title: Concatenation of Array
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums of length n, return an array ans of length 2n where ans[i] == nums[i] and ans[i + n] == nums[i] for 0 <= i < n (i.e. ans is the concatenation of two nums arrays).

examples.explanation: The array ans is formed by concatenating nums with itself.

hints: The result array's length is always exactly double the input's length.
Every element needs to appear at TWO positions in the result: its own index, and its own index plus n.
You don't need any conditional logic — a single pass filling both halves at once works.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Create the result array, then run two separate loops: one to copy nums into the first half, another to copy nums into the second half.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) (for the output array, not counting it as extra since it's the required result)

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Combine both copies into a single pass — for each index i, set both ans[i] and ans[i + n] at once. Same complexity, one loop instead of two.

sections.title: Richest Customer Wealth
sections.problemStatement: You are given an m x n integer grid accounts where accounts[i][j] is the amount of money the i-th customer has in the j-th bank. Return the wealth that the richest customer has. A customer's wealth is the sum of all their bank accounts.

examples.explanation: 1st customer has wealth = 1+2+3 = 6. 2nd customer has wealth = 3+2+1 = 6. Both are richest with 6.
2nd customer has wealth 7+3=10, which is the maximum.

hints: This is really two nested things: sum each row (2D Arrays), then find the maximum of those sums (1D Arrays' max-finding pattern).
You don't need to store every row's sum — just track the best one seen so far as you go.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Compute every row's sum into a separate array first, then scan that array for the maximum in a second pass.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Track the maximum directly while summing each row — no need to store every row's sum in a separate array.

sections.title: Shuffle the Array
sections.problemStatement: Given the array nums consisting of 2n elements in the form [x1,x2,...,xn,y1,y2,...,yn], return the array in the form [x1,y1,x2,y2,...,xn,yn].
hints: x's values live at indices 0..n-1, and y's values live at indices n..2n-1 in the original array.
For each i from 0 to n-1, the result needs nums[i] followed immediately by nums[i+n].

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Split nums into two separate arrays x and y first, then build the result by alternating between them.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the extra x and y arrays

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Build the result directly from nums, without allocating intermediate x and y arrays.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space (beyond the required output)

sections.title: Find Numbers with Even Number of Digits
sections.problemStatement: Given an array nums of integers, return how many of them contain an even number of digits.

examples.explanation: 12 has 2 digits (even). 345 has 3 (odd). 2 has 1 (odd). 6 has 1 (odd). 7896 has 4 (even). Count: 2.

hints: Converting each number to a String (String Methods) lets you use .length() directly to count digits.
A number's digit count can also be computed purely numerically, without ever creating a String — repeatedly divide by 10 and count steps.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Convert each number to a String and check if its length is even.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * d), where d is the average number of digits
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(d) per String conversion, not counting the fixed output

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Count digits purely numerically, avoiding String conversion entirely.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * d), where d is the average number of digits

sections.title: Max Consecutive Ones
sections.problemStatement: Given a binary array nums, return the maximum number of consecutive 1's in the array.

examples.explanation: The first two digits or the last three digits are consecutive 1s. The maximum number of consecutive 1s is 3.

hints: Track a running streak of consecutive 1s, resetting to 0 the moment you hit a 0.
Keep a separate 'best streak so far' variable, updated whenever the current streak beats it.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every starting index, count how far the run of 1s extends, tracking the best found. (Shown for comparison — this is more work than needed.)
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n) amortized (each element is visited a bounded number of times), though it looks nested

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A single pass with a running streak counter, reset on every 0 -- the standard, cleanest solution.
